statement: [arXiv:2408.06171 Theorem 5.3] Let Γ be a simple graph, let Γ1,Γ2⊆Γ be subgraphs, and for each vertex v∈Γ let (Mv,τv) be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace. Let (MΓ,τ)=∗_{v,Γ}(Mv,τv) be the tracial graph product, and for every subgraph Λ⊆Γ write MΛ for the canonical graph-product subalgebra, with M∅=C1. Let P⊆MΓ be a von Neumann subalgebra. If P is amenable relative to MΓ1 inside MΓ and P is amenable relative to MΓ2 inside MΓ, then P is amenable relative to MΓ1∩Γ2 inside MΓ. Here P amenable relative to Q⊆MΓ means that there is a P-central positive functional on 1P⟨MΓ,eQ⟩1P whose restriction to 1PMΓ1P is the trace.
proof_sketch: Set Qi=MΓi for i=1,2 and Q0=Q1∩Q2=MΓ1∩Γ2. The relative amenability assumptions imply, by the Popa-Vaes bimodule characterization, that the standard MΓ-P bimodule L2(MΓ) is weakly contained in H=L2(⟨MΓ,eQ1⟩)⊗_{MΓ}L2(⟨MΓ,eQ2⟩). The main work is to identify H with a bimodule weakly contained in the relative coarse bimodule over Q0. Use the reduced-word decomposition of graph products. Let V be the set of Coxeter words that do not start with letters from Γ1 and do not end with letters from Γ2. A dense subspace of H is spanned by vectors x eQ1 y ⊗_{MΓ} eQ2 z with x,z∈MΓ and y∈Ṁv for v∈V. The key conditional-expectation computation, coming from the graph-product normal form, is that for x,x′∈MΓ and y,y′∈Ṁv, EQ1(y* EQ2(x*x′) y′)=τ(y*y′) EQ0∩Link(v)(x*x′)=EQ0(y* EQ0(x*x′) y′). Hence the inner product of such spanning vectors in H agrees exactly with the inner product of x⊗_{Q0}y⊗_{Q0}z in a relative tensor product over Q0. This gives an isometric MΓ-MΓ bimodular identification of a dense subspace of H with a sub-bimodule built from L2(MΓ)⊗_{Q0}L2(MΓ). Therefore L2(MΓ), as an MΓ-P bimodule, is weakly contained in the Q0-relative coarse bimodule, which is precisely the bimodule criterion for P to be amenable relative to Q0.

statement: [arXiv:2408.06171 Lemma 8.10] Let Γ be a simple graph with |Γ|≥3 and assume that StarΓ(v)≠Γ for every vertex v, where StarΓ(v)={v}∪LinkΓ(v). For every v∈Γ let (Mv,τv) be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace, and assume that Mv contains a unitary uv with τv(uv)=0. Let MΓ=∗_{v,Γ}(Mv,τv) be the tracial graph product. Then for every strict subgraph Λ⊊Γ, one has MΓ ⊀_{MΓ} MΛ in Popa's intertwining sense. Equivalently, for every Λ⊊Γ there is a sequence of unitaries (Un)n in MΓ such that ∥EMΛ(x Un y)∥2→0 for all x,y∈MΓ.
proof_sketch: Fix Λ⊊Γ and choose a vertex v∈Γ∖Λ. Because |Γ|≥3 and no vertex has Star equal to Γ, the corresponding right-angled Coxeter group WΓ has an infinite conjugacy class for v. Choose words sn∈WΓ so that |sn^{-1} v sn|≥2n+1. By deleting initial letters of sn that commute with v, one may also arrange that every letter at the start of sn fails to commute with v; then sn^{-1} v sn is reduced and |sn|≥n. Write sn^{-1} v sn as a reduced word (w1,...,wk) in vertices and form the graph-product unitary Un=uw1⋯uwk, where each uwi is the fixed trace-zero unitary in Mwi. Then Un lies in the reduced word space Ṁ_{sn^{-1} v sn}. The key combinatorial input is Lemma 8.8: if a and b are reduced operators supported on words of length at most n, then EMΛ(a Un b)=0. Intuitively, the long conjugate sn^{-1} v sn contains a protected occurrence of the vertex v outside Λ; multiplying on the left and right by words of length at most n cannot cancel it into a word entirely supported in Λ. Now approximate arbitrary x,y∈MΓ in L2-norm by finite sums x0,y0 of reduced operators, all with word length at most n for n large. For such n, EMΛ(x0 Un y0)=0, and since Un is unitary and EMΛ is L2-contractive, ∥EMΛ(x Un y)∥2≤∥(x−x0)Un y∥2+∥x0 Un(y−y0)∥2 can be made arbitrarily small. This sequence (Un)n witnesses the failure of Popa intertwining.

statement: [arXiv:2408.06171 Theorem 8.11] Let Γ be an irreducible finite simple graph with |Γ|≥3, where irreducible means that Γ cannot be written as Γ=Γ1∪Γ2 with Γ1,Γ2 non-empty subgraphs and LinkΓ(Γ1)=Γ2. For every v∈Γ let (Mv,τv) be a von Neumann algebra, Mv≠C, with normal faithful trace, and assume that Mv contains a unitary uv satisfying τv(uv)=0. Then the tracial graph product MΓ=∗_{v,Γ}(Mv,τv) is a prime II1 factor; that is, MΓ is a II1 factor and it admits no tensor decomposition MΓ=N1⊗N2 with N1,N2 diffuse von Neumann algebras.
proof_sketch: First, the trace-zero unitaries and the structural theorem of Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson imply that MΓ is a II1 factor. Irreducibility with |Γ|≥3 implies StarΓ(v)≠Γ for every v: otherwise Γ={v}∪Link(v) is a non-trivial join decomposition. Hence Lemma 8.10 applies and gives MΓ⊀_{MΓ}MΛ for every strict subgraph Λ⊊Γ. The paper proves a general primeness criterion, Lemma 8.3: for a finite irreducible graph, if MΓ is a II1 factor and MΓ does not intertwine into any strict graph subalgebra, then MΓ is either prime or amenable. The proof of that criterion is as follows. If MΓ=N1⊗N2 with N1,N2 diffuse, then the normalizer of N1 is all of MΓ. A minimal-support argument using the quasi-normalizer control of Proposition 5.8 shows that N1, and similarly any diffuse amenable subalgebra A⊆N2, cannot intertwine into any strict graph subalgebra. For each vertex v, decompose MΓ as the amalgamated free product MStar(v) *_{MLink(v)} MΓ∖{v}. Vaes's normalizer theorem for amalgamated free products applied to the amenable algebra A gives three alternatives: A embeds into MLink(v), its normalizer embeds into one of the two free-product sides, or its normalizer is amenable relative to MLink(v). The first two alternatives are impossible by the non-intertwining just obtained, so the normalizer is amenable relative to MLink(v) for every v. Iterating Theorem 5.3 over all vertices gives amenability relative to M_{∩v Link(v)}=C, hence the normalizer is amenable; this forces N1 amenable, and symmetrically N2 amenable, so MΓ is amenable. Thus non-primeness implies amenability. Finally MΓ is not amenable: by the graph-product amenability criterion, amenability would force either Γ to be complete or, for a non-edge {v,w}, Link({v,w})=Γ∖{v,w} with both corresponding algebras two-dimensional; either case gives a non-trivial join decomposition, contradicting irreducibility and |Γ|≥3. Therefore MΓ is non-amenable, so the prime-or-amenable alternative yields primeness.

statement: [arXiv:2204.00517 Theorem 6.2] Let (M,τ) be a tracial von Neumann algebra, let X⊂B(L²M) be an M-boundary piece, meaning a nonzero hereditary C*-subalgebra whose multiplier algebra meets M and JMJ ultraweakly densely, and let B⊂M be a von Neumann subalgebra. Put K_X^{∞,1}(M) for the ∥·∥∞,1-closure of X, where ∥T∥∞,1 is the norm of T as a map M⊂L²M→L¹M, equivalently ∥T∥∞,1=sup{|⟨T â,b̂⟩|: a,b∈M, ∥a∥≤1, ∥b∥≤1}. Define S_X(M)={T∈B(L²M): [T,JxJ]∈K_X^{∞,1}(M) for every x∈M}. Then the following are equivalent. (1) There exists a B-central state φ on S_X(M) such that φ restricted to M is normal. (2) There exists a nonzero projection p∈Z(B) and a B-central state φ on S_X(M) such that φ(x)=τ(x)/τ(p) for every x∈pMp. (3) There exists a nonzero projection p∈Z(B) and a pMp-bimodular unital completely positive map Φ:S_X(M)→⟨pMp,e_{Bp}⟩, where ⟨pMp,e_{Bp}⟩ is the Jones basic construction for Bp⊂pMp. (4) For every normal operator M-system E such that there exists a state φ₀∈(E^♮)_{X-prox} with φ₀ restricted to M equal to τ, there exists a B-central state φ on E such that φ restricted to M is normal. Here E^♮ is the bimodule dual, identified with M-bimodular completely bounded maps E→B(L²M), and (E^♮)_{X-prox} means that [x,φ₀] is X-mixing for every x∈M. The inclusion B⊂M is called properly proximal relative to X precisely when these equivalent conditions fail; in particular, M is properly proximal when B=M and X=K(L²M).
proof_sketch: The equivalence of (1), (2), and (3) is the standard relative-amenability/basic-construction criterion: a B-central state on an M-operator system whose restriction to M is normal can be cut by a nonzero central projection p∈Z(B), normalized on pMp, and represented via the GNS/Stinespring construction as a pMp-bimodular u.c.p. map into the basic construction ⟨pMp,e_{Bp}⟩; conversely composing such a map with the canonical vector/trace state on the basic construction gives the required B-central state. For (4)⇒(1), take E=S_X(M) and φ₀(T)=⟨T 1̂,1̂⟩. By the definition of S_X(M) and Theorem 5.6/Remark 5.8, commutators with JMJ lie in the X-mixing part, so φ₀ is X-proximal. For (1)⇒(4), let E and φ₀ be as in (4). The state φ₀ corresponds to an M-bimodular u.c.p. map Φ₀:E→B(L²M) with φ₀(T)=⟨Φ₀(T)1̂,1̂⟩. X-proximality of φ₀ forces Φ₀(E)⊂S_X(M): for T∈E, x,a,b∈M and any uniformly bounded net μ_i in M⊗_Con M^op converging in the X-topology, one computes ⟨μ_i(x·Φ₀(T)−Φ₀(T)·x) â,b̂⟩=μ_i([x,φ₀])(b*Ta), which converges because [x,φ₀] is X-mixing. Thus [Φ₀(T),Jx*J] is X-mixing, hence in K_X^{∞,1}. Composing the B-central state on S_X(M) supplied by (1) with Φ₀ gives a B-central state on E whose restriction to M is normal.

statement: [arXiv:2204.00517 Lemma 6.1] Let (M,τ) be a tracial von Neumann algebra, fix an M-boundary piece X⊂B(L²M), and let V be a dual normal operator M-bimodule. Let V_{X-mix} denote the norm-closed subspace of X-mixing vectors, i.e. those ξ∈V for which the orbit map μ↦μ(ξ), from the convolution algebra M⊗_Con M^op with the X-topology to V with its weak-* topology, is continuous on bounded sets. For ξ∈V define M₀(ξ)={x∈M: [x,ξ]∈V_{X-mix} and [x*,ξ]∈V_{X-mix}}, where [x,ξ]=xξ−ξx is computed using the given M-bimodule structure. Then M₀(ξ) is a von Neumann subalgebra of M. Consequently, ξ is X-proximal, meaning [x,ξ]∈V_{X-mix} for every x∈M, if and only if M₀(ξ) is ultraweakly dense in M.
proof_sketch: First M₀(ξ) is a unital *-subalgebra. The key identity for products is [xy,ξ]=x[y,ξ]+[x,ξ]y, and V_{X-mix} is an M-bimodule; the condition is made self-adjoint by explicitly requiring the same for x*. The only non-formal point is ultraweak closedness. If x is in the ultraweak closure of M₀(ξ), take a uniformly bounded net x_i∈M₀(ξ) with x_i→x ultraweakly. Then [x_i,ξ]−[x,ξ]=(x_i−x)ξ−ξ(x_i−x) tends to 0 in the M-M topology on V. Proposition 5.1 says V_{X-mix} is a strong operator M-bimodule, equivalently closed for this M-M topology. Hence [x,ξ]∈V_{X-mix}; applying the same argument to x_i*→x* gives [x*,ξ]∈V_{X-mix}. Thus M₀(ξ) is ultraweakly closed, hence a von Neumann subalgebra. The final assertion is immediate: if M₀(ξ) is ultraweakly dense, closedness gives M₀(ξ)=M, which is exactly X-proximality; the converse is tautological. In applications to S_X(M), take V=B(L²M) with the M-bimodule structure x·T·y=Jy*JTJx*J. Theorem 5.6 identifies the X-mixing vectors in this module with K_X^{∞,1}(M), so Lemma 6.1 becomes the practical generator test: to prove T∈S_X(M), it suffices to check [T,JxJ]∈K_X^{∞,1}(M) for an ultraweakly generating *-subalgebra of M.

statement: [arXiv:2204.00517 Proposition 6.7] Let M₁ and M₂ be diffuse tracial von Neumann algebras, and let M=M₁*M₂ be their tracial free product. Then M is properly proximal. Equivalently, for S(M)={T∈B(L²M): [T,JxJ]∈K^{∞,1}(M) for every x∈M}, where K^{∞,1}(M) is the ∥·∥∞,1-closure of the compact operators K(L²M), there is no M-central state on S(M) whose restriction to M is normal.
proof_sketch: Use the reduced-word decomposition L²(M)=C1̂⊕⊕_{n≥1}⊕_{i₁≠⋯≠i_n} H^0_{i₁}⊗⋯⊗H^0_{i_n}, where H_i=L²(M_i) and H_i^0=H_i⊖C1̂. Let H_ℓ(1)=C1̂⊕span of reduced words whose first letter is not from M₁, and let P be the projection onto H_ℓ(1)⊥, i.e. onto words beginning with M₁. Define Q analogously as the projection onto words beginning with M₂. A reduced-word calculation shows P∈S(M): if z∈M₂ with τ(z)=0, right multiplication JzJ preserves both H_ℓ(1) and its orthogonal complement, so [P,JzJ]=0; if z∈M₁ with τ(z)=0, JzJ preserves H_ℓ(1)⊖C1̂ and the only possible crossing is through the vacuum, giving [P,JzJ]=[JzJ,Proj_{C1̂}], a finite-rank operator. Lemma 6.1 then extends this check from the generating trace-zero parts of M₁ and M₂ to all of M. Similarly Q∈S(M). Since M₂ is diffuse, choose trace-zero unitaries u₁,u₂∈U(M₂) that are orthogonal in L², i.e. τ(u_i)=0 and τ(u₁* u₂)=0. Reduced-word orthogonality gives u₁*Pu₁+u₂*Pu₂≤Q. Since M₁ is diffuse, choose a trace-zero unitary v∈U(M₁); then v*Qv≤P. If φ were an M-central state on S(M), centrality under these unitaries would give 2φ(P)=φ(u₁*Pu₁)+φ(u₂*Pu₂)≤φ(Q) and φ(Q)=φ(v*Qv)≤φ(P). Hence φ(P)=φ(Q)=0. Now 1−P−Q is the vacuum projection Proj_{C1̂}. Centrality and diffuseness imply φ(Proj_{C1̂})=0: choose arbitrarily many pairwise L²-orthogonal unitaries w_k in one diffuse free-product component; the projections w_k Proj_{C1̂} w_k* are pairwise orthogonal and all have φ-value equal to φ(Proj_{C1̂}), forcing that value to be 0. Thus φ(1)=φ(P)+φ(Q)+φ(Proj_{C1̂})=0, a contradiction. Therefore no such central state exists, and M is properly proximal.

statement: [arXiv:1411.2799 Theorem 2.26] Let Γ be a simplicial graph. For every vertex u of Γ let (M_u,ω_u) be a von Neumann algebra with a faithful normal state, and for every full subgraph Λ⊆Γ let (M_Λ,ω_Λ) denote the graph product von Neumann algebra over Λ with its graph product state; by convention M_∅=C. Fix a vertex v. Let Link(v) be the full subgraph on the vertices adjacent to v, let Star(v)=Link(v)∪{v}, and put M1=M_{Star(v)}, N=M_{Link(v)}, and M2=M_{Γ\{v}}. Via the canonical inclusions one has N⊂M1⊂M_Γ and N⊂M2⊂M_Γ, and the graph product state gives canonical faithful normal conditional expectations E1:M1→N and E2:M2→N. Then there is a unique normal ∗-isomorphism π:M1 *_N M2 → M_Γ, where M1 *_N M2 is the von Neumann algebraic amalgamated free product with respect to E1 and E2, such that π restricts to the canonical inclusion on M1 and on M2. Moreover π is state-preserving: the amalgamated free product state is carried to the graph product state on M_Γ.
proof_sketch: Use the universal property of the von Neumann amalgamated free product. M_Γ is generated by M1 and M2, so it remains to prove freeness over N with respect to the canonical expectation E:M_Γ→N. Let M_k^∘=ker(E|_{M_k}). It suffices, by weak density of reduced operators, to check E(a1...an)=0 for alternating products with a_j∈M_{l_j}^∘, l_j∈{1,2}, l_j≠l_{j+1}. Approximate each a_j by a reduced operator x^j_1...x^j_{r_j}, whose associated reduced word lies in Γ_{l_j} and contains at least one vertex outside Link(v). Concatenate the associated words. Induct on total length r_1+...+r_n. If the concatenated word is reduced, then the product is a reduced operator whose word contains a vertex outside Link(v), hence E kills it. If it is not reduced, after commuting adjacent letters along edges and using that each block-word is already reduced, the first possible cancellation occurs across two adjacent alternating blocks. The equal vertex w must lie in Γ_1∩Γ_2=Link(v). Write the product of the two adjacent M_w-letters as P_w(xy)+ω_w(xy)1 with P_w(z)=z−ω_w(z)1. This rewrites E(a1...an) as a sum of expectations of alternating products with strictly smaller total word length (possibly after merging the adjacent blocks), so the induction hypothesis gives zero. Thus M1 and M2 are free over N inside M_Γ. The universal property gives a normal ∗-homomorphism M1 *_N M2→M_Γ. It is onto because M1 and M2 generate M_Γ, and injective/state-preserving because both sides are represented in their faithful GNS representations for the same free product state.

statement: [arXiv:1411.2799 Remarks 2.23 and 2.24] Let Γ be a simplicial graph and let (M_u,ω_u)_{u∈VΓ} be von Neumann algebras with faithful normal states. Let M_Γ be their graph product von Neumann algebra with graph product state ω_Γ. For a full subgraph Λ⊆Γ, the graph product M_Λ identifies canonically and state-preservingly with the von Neumann subalgebra of M_Γ generated by the vertex algebras M_u for u∈VΛ. Under this identification there is a unique normal conditional expectation E_Λ:M_Γ→M_Λ preserving the graph product state and satisfying the following rule on reduced operators: if a=a1...an with a_i∈M_{v_i}, ω_{v_i}(a_i)=0, and (v_1,...,v_n) a reduced word in Γ, then E_Λ(a)=a when all v_i belong to Λ, and E_Λ(a)=0 when at least one v_i is outside Λ. In particular, for every vertex u there is a unique state-preserving normal conditional expectation E_u:M_Γ→M_u killing all reduced operators not lying in M_u. Also, if all pairs of vertices of Γ are connected by edges, then M_Γ is the spatial tensor product of the M_u with product state; if Γ has no edges, then M_Γ is the free product of the (M_u,ω_u). Consequently, for every vertex v, multiplication gives a canonical state-preserving isomorphism M_{Star(v)}≅M_v ⊗ M_{Link(v)} (spatial von Neumann tensor product), sending x⊗y to xy.
proof_sketch: The canonical embedding of M_Λ into M_Γ follows from the graph product universal property: the subalgebra generated by the vertex algebras in Λ has exactly the same commutation relations and reduced-word moment vanishing as the graph product over Λ, and the graph product state is faithful. For the expectation, use the Fock-space model H_Γ=CΩ⊕⊕_{w∈W_min(Γ)} H_w. Let H_Λ be the closed subspace spanned by Ω and by those reduced-word summands H_w whose vertices all lie in Λ. The orthogonal projection P_Λ:H_Γ→H_Λ implements E_Λ by E_Λ(x)=P_Λ x P_Λ, after identifying H_Λ with L^2(M_Λ). On the dense ∗-algebra spanned by 1 and reduced operators, this compression is exactly the stated rule: reduced words entirely in Λ remain unchanged, while any word containing a vertex outside Λ sends Ω into an orthogonal summand and is killed by compression. Normality, state preservation, and uniqueness follow from weak density of the reduced operators and faithfulness of the GNS vector. The complete-graph and edgeless cases are immediate from the defining commutation relations and reduced-word moment formula: complete graphs impose full commutation, giving the tensor product; edgeless graphs impose no commutations, giving Voiculescu free product. Since v is adjacent to every vertex of Link(v), the vertex algebra M_v commutes with M_{Link(v)} inside M_{Star(v)}, and the preceding complete-cross-commutation argument identifies the star algebra with M_v ⊗ M_{Link(v)} even though Link(v) itself need not be complete.

statement: [arXiv:1411.2799 Proposition 2.25] Let Γ be a simplicial graph and let (M_u,ω_u)_{u∈VΓ} be von Neumann algebras with faithful normal states. For full subgraphs Γ0,Γ1⊆Γ, regard M_{Γ0} and M_{Γ1} as the canonical graph product von Neumann subalgebras of M_Γ. Then M_{Γ0} ∩ M_{Γ1} = M_{Γ0∩Γ1}, where Γ0∩Γ1 is the full subgraph on VΓ0∩VΓ1.
proof_sketch: The inclusion M_{Γ0∩Γ1}⊂M_{Γ0}∩M_{Γ1} is immediate from the canonical subgraph inclusions. For the reverse inclusion, use the reduced-word/Fock decomposition. The L^2-space of M_Λ is the closed span of Ω and reduced-word summands whose letters all lie in Λ. Hence L^2(M_{Γ0})∩L^2(M_{Γ1}) is exactly L^2(M_{Γ0∩Γ1}), because a reduced word belongs to both spans precisely when all of its vertices lie in both subgraphs. If x∈M_{Γ0}∩M_{Γ1}, then xΩ lies in both L^2(M_{Γ0}) and L^2(M_{Γ1}), hence in L^2(M_{Γ0∩Γ1}). Let E_{Γ0∩Γ1}:M_Γ→M_{Γ0∩Γ1} be the canonical state-preserving expectation. Its L^2-implementation is the orthogonal projection onto L^2(M_{Γ0∩Γ1}), so E_{Γ0∩Γ1}(x)Ω=xΩ. The graph product state is faithful, so Ω is separating for M_Γ; therefore x=E_{Γ0∩Γ1}(x)∈M_{Γ0∩Γ1}. Equivalently, the paper proves the same fact first on the algebraic span of reduced operators: write an element of M_{Γ0} as scalar plus finitely many reduced operators, apply E_{Γ1}, and observe that only those reduced words with all letters in Γ0∩Γ1 survive; weak closure then gives the von Neumann algebra equality.

statement: [arXiv:2404.08150 Theorem A] Let G=(V,E) be a finite simple graph. For each v∈V let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra, and let (M,τ)=graph product over G of the family (M_v,τ_v). For U⊆V, write M_U for the von Neumann subalgebra of M generated by the vertex algebras with vertices in U, equivalently the graph product over the induced subgraph G|_U. Assume that for every v∈V the algebra M_v contains a unitary u_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then for any V_1,V_2⊆V, M_{V_1} is amenable relative to M_{V_2} inside M, i.e. there exists a conditional expectation Φ:⟨M,e_{M_{V_2}}⟩→M_{V_1} whose restriction to M is normal, if and only if both of the following conditions hold: (1) M_v is amenable for every v∈V_1\V_2. (2) For every v∈V_1\V_2 and every w∈V_1 with w≠v, either v and w are adjacent in G, or both of the following hold: (a) dim(M_v)=dim(M_w)=2; (b) v and w are adjacent to every vertex in V_1\{v,w}.
proof_sketch: The paper proves a more general statial version (Theorem C) and then specializes to traces. The tracial simplification uses the dichotomy for free products with trace-zero unitaries: for two tracial algebras M_1,M_2, the following are equivalent: dim M_1=dim M_2=2; M_1∗M_2 is amenable; M_1∗M_2 is amenable relative to one free factor. The implication from relative amenability to amenability uses Proposition 4.2 (non-intertwining of the free product into a factor) plus Ioana's relative amenability/intertwining result; amenability of the 2-dimensional case is Ching/Dykema. For sufficiency, list all non-edge pairs P_j={v,w} in V_1 with v∈V_1\V_2. Condition (2b) forces these exceptional pairs to be mutually disjoint and to commute with all remaining vertices; vertices in Q_1=V_1\(V_2∪⋃P_j) commute with all of V_1. Hence M_{V_1} decomposes as a tensor product of the M_{P_j}, of M_{Q_1}, and of the remaining M_{V_1∩V_2}-part. Each M_{P_j} is amenable relative to M_{P_j∩V_2} by the 2-dimensional free product dichotomy, and M_{Q_1} is amenable by condition (1) and Connes amenability stability under tensor products. Lemma C.3 tensors these relative amenability inclusions, giving M_{V_1} amenable relative to M_{V_1∩V_2}; Lemma C.1, using the commuting-square conditional expectations for induced subgraphs, promotes this to relative amenability to M_{V_2} inside M. For necessity, relative amenability implies the correspondence weak containment L^2(M) ≺ L^2(M)⊗_{M_{V_2}}L^2(M) as M_{V_1}-M_{V_1} bimodules. The bimodule decomposition/fusion rules of Theorem D yield Lemma 6.2: every subgraph contained in V_1\V_2 gives an amenable algebra, and every factorial M_{V_0}, V_0⊆V_1, is amenable relative to some M_U with U⊆V_0∩V_2. Applying this to V_0={v} gives condition (1). For a non-adjacent pair v∈V_1\V_2, w∈V_1, apply it to V_0={v,w}; if the free product M_v∗M_w is a factor (the Ueda case, i.e. not both dimension 2), one obtains amenability relative to either C or M_w, contradicting the tracial free-product dichotomy unless both dimensions are 2. Finally, if some u∈V_1\{v,w} is not adjacent to v or w, then the induced graph on {v,w,u} is join-irreducible, so Theorem 2.4 makes M_{v,w,u} a factor; Lemma 6.2 gives relative amenability to a subalgebra contained in M_{w,u}. But M_{v,w,u} is an amalgamated free product over M_u, and Lemma C.2, using the trace-zero unitaries, forbids such relative amenability. Thus v and w must be adjacent to all other vertices in V_1.

statement: [arXiv:2404.08150 Lemma 3.17] Let G=(V,E) be a finite simple graph, let (M_v,φ_v) be von Neumann algebras with faithful normal states, and let (M,φ) be their graph product. For U⊆V write M_U for the induced-subgraph subalgebra and E_{M_U}:M→M_U for the φ-preserving conditional expectation. A word w=w_1...w_m in V is G-reduced relative to (V_1,V_2) if it is G-reduced and, for every a∈V_1 and b∈V_2, the words a w and w b are G-reduced. Let V_1,V_2⊆V. Let w=w_1...w_m and ~w=~w_1...~w_n be G-reduced words relative to (V_1,V_2). Choose x_j∈M_{w_j}∩ker φ_{w_j} and ~x_j∈M_{~w_j}∩ker φ_{~w_j}, and put x=x_1...x_m and ~x=~x_1...~x_n, with the convention that the empty product is 1. Let U be the set of vertices v∈V_1∩V_2 that are adjacent in G to every letter of w; if w is empty, U=V_1∩V_2. Then for every y∈M_{V_1}, E_{M_{V_2}}(~x^* y x)=φ(~x^* x) E_{M_U}(y). In particular, if w and ~w are not equivalent reduced words, equivalently cannot be obtained from one another by swapping adjacent commuting letters, then E_{M_{V_2}}(~x^* y x)=0 for all y∈M_{V_1}.
proof_sketch: It is enough to test the desired equality against arbitrary z∈M_{V_2}, i.e. prove φ(~x^* y x z)=φ(~x^*x)φ(E_{M_U}(y)z). By the reduced-word spanning theorem for graph products, reduce z to a product z_1...z_l of centered vertex elements indexed by a G-reduced word in V_2. Using the relative factorization lemma (Lemma 3.12), rewrite this word up to admissible swaps as a product a^(1)a^(2), where a^(1) is a reduced word in U and a^(2) is reduced relative to U on the left. Since vertices in U commute with all letters of w, the corresponding factor z^(1)∈M_U can be moved across x, so it suffices to replace y by y z^(1) and assume z itself is indexed by a word reduced relative to (U,∅). Similarly reduce y to a centered reduced word b in V_1. Lemma 3.11 then says b w a is G-reduced, where a is the word for z. Lemma 3.13 gives the key uniqueness: because w and ~w are both reduced relative to (V_1,V_2), the only way the reduced word b w a can be equivalent to ~w is for b and a to be empty and w equivalent to ~w. Now apply the basic graph-product inner-product formula (Lemma 3.15): inequivalent reduced words are φ-orthogonal, and equivalent ones have inner product equal to the product of vertex inner products. There are three cases. If a=b=empty, the formula is exactly φ(~x^*x). If b is nonempty and a empty, then the left side is zero by inequivalence, while the right side is zero because y is a centered reduced word, hence φ(E_{M_U}(y))=φ(y)=0. If a is nonempty, the left side is again zero by inequivalence, and the right side is zero because z is orthogonal to M_U by the conditional expectation formula for reduced words. This proves the tested identity for spanning y,z and hence by normality/density for all y∈M_{V_1}.

statement: [arXiv:2404.08150 Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5 (Theorem D)] Let G=(V,E) be a finite simple graph, let (M_v,φ_v) be von Neumann algebras with faithful normal states, and let (M,φ) be their graph product. For U⊆V write M_U for the induced-subgraph subalgebra. For V_1,V_2⊆V and U⊆V_1∩V_2, let W_G(V_1,V_2,U) be a set of representatives of equivalence classes of G-reduced words w=w_1...w_l which are reduced relative to (V_1,V_2) and satisfy U={v∈V_1∩V_2: v is adjacent to every w_j}. For the empty word, this set condition gives U=V_1∩V_2. Define the multiplicity k_G(V_1,V_2,U)=Σ_{w_1...w_l∈W_G(V_1,V_2,U)} Π_{j=1}^l (dim L^2(M_{w_j},φ_{w_j})−1), with empty product equal to 1. Then, as an M_{V_1}-M_{V_2} correspondence, L^2(M,φ) is unitarily isomorphic to the direct sum over U⊆V_1∩V_2 of (L^2(M_{V_1},φ_{V_1}) ⊗_{M_U} L^2(M_{V_2},φ_{V_2})) with multiplicity k_G(V_1,V_2,U), where ⊗_{M_U} denotes Connes fusion. Moreover, if H_U(A,B):= L^2(M_A,φ_A) ⊗_{M_U} L^2(M_B,φ_B) for U⊆A∩B, then for V_1,V_2,V_3⊆V, U_1⊆V_1∩V_2, and U_2⊆V_2∩V_3, one has the fusion rule H_{U_1}(V_1,V_2) ⊗_{M_{V_2}} H_{U_2}(V_2,V_3) ≅ direct sum over W⊆U_1∩U_2 of H_W(V_1,V_3) with multiplicity k_{G|_{V_2}}(U_1,U_2,W), where the multiplicity is computed in the induced graph on V_2.
proof_sketch: Choose for each vertex v an orthonormal basis B_v of L^2(M_v,φ_v)⊖C1. The graph-product Fock-space description gives continuous multilinear reduced-word vectors ξ_1...ξ_l∈L^2(M) for ξ_j∈L^2(M_{w_j})⊖C1; inequivalent reduced words are orthogonal, and equivalent words have the expected permuted tensor inner product. The key cyclic-bimodule computation is Lemma 5.3, proved from Lemma 3.17: if w is reduced relative to (V_1,V_2), ξ=ξ_1...ξ_l is a unit vector over w, and U is the set of common vertices adjacent to all letters of w, then the M_{V_1}-M_{V_2} bimodule generated by ξ is unitarily L^2(M_{V_1})⊗_{M_U}L^2(M_{V_2}), with ξ sent to 1⊗1. The verification compares inner products of a·ξ·b with ξ for a∈M_{V_1} and analytic b∈M_{V_2}; Lemma 3.17 gives exactly the conditional expectation E_{M_{V_2}}(x^* a x)=E_{M_U}(a), after normalizing ξ. Orthogonality of distinct summands follows from the same lemma and the reduced-word inner product formula. Density is obtained by taking any reduced word and applying the relative factorization lemma (Lemma 3.12): up to admissible swaps it factors as a word in V_1, followed by a word reduced relative to (V_1,V_2), followed by a word in V_2. Hence every reduced-word vector lies in the span of the cyclic bimodules above. Counting choices of basis vectors over representatives of relative reduced-word classes gives the stated multiplicity. For the fusion rule, first cancel the middle identity correspondence: H_{U_1}(V_1,V_2)⊗_{M_{V_2}}H_{U_2}(V_2,V_3) identifies with L^2(M_{V_1})⊗_{M_{U_1}}L^2(M_{V_2})⊗_{M_{U_2}}L^2(M_{V_3}). Apply the just-proved decomposition theorem to the middle correspondence M_{U_1}L^2(M_{V_2})_{M_{U_2}}, but now inside the induced graph G|_{V_2}. This decomposes it as a direct sum of L^2(M_{U_1})⊗_{M_W}L^2(M_{U_2}) with multiplicities k_{G|_{V_2}}(U_1,U_2,W). Fusing the outer L^2(M_{V_1}) and L^2(M_{V_3}) factors over M_{U_1} and M_{U_2} gives exactly H_W(V_1,V_3).

statement: [arXiv:2107.02917 Corollary 3.2] Let G1 and G2 be countable groups containing a common subgroup H, and let G = G1 *_H G2 be their amalgamated free product. Assume that, after possibly interchanging G1 and G2, one has [G1 : H] ≥ 3 and [G2 : H] ≥ 2. For a subgroup H ≤ G, let c_0(G,H) be the C*-subalgebra of ℓ∞(G) consisting of bounded functions f such that f(g_n) → 0 whenever the sequence g_n eventually leaves every double coset sHt, s,t ∈ G. Then G is properly proximal relative to H: equivalently, there is no left G-invariant state on the right-translation fixed-point algebra (((ℓ∞(G)/c_0(G,H))^{**})^{G_r}).
proof_sketch: The paper derives this from Proposition 3.1 by applying it to the Bass-Serre tree T of G1 *_H G2. Vertices are cosets G/G1 and G/G2, edges are cosets G/H, and edge stabilizers are conjugates of H. The index assumptions make T infinite and make the action on the Bowditch compactification ΔT non-elementary. Proposition 3.1 gives relative north-south dynamics for every sequence escaping all edge-stabilizer double cosets; with a diffuse measure on the perfect limit set, this gives relative proper proximality. Since edge stabilizers are conjugates of H, relative proper proximality with respect to edge stabilizers is the same as relative proper proximality with respect to H. The paper also gives a direct normal-form proof, which is often the useful portable argument. Choose right-coset transversals T1 for H\G1 and T2 for H\G2. In the quotient ℓ∞(G)/c_0(G,H), let p1 and p2 be the characteristic functions of elements whose reduced amalgamated normal form begins, respectively, with a nontrivial letter from the first or second side. The omission of elements of H is harmless because 1_H ∈ c_0(G,H), so p1+p2=1 in the quotient. A key claim is that p1 and p2 are right G-invariant modulo c_0(G,H): if multiplication on the right by a fixed g changes the first side along a sequence g_n escaping all H-double cosets, then normal-form cancellation forces the non-H tail of g_n to come from a finite set, contradicting relative escape. Now choose two elements a1,a2 in the side with at least three H-cosets, representing two distinct non-H cosets, and one element b in the other side outside H. If p is the projection for words beginning in the side opposite a1,a2 and q the other one, then left multiplication gives disjoint inclusions a1 p + a2 p ≤ q and b q ≤ p in the quotient. Any left G-invariant state φ on the right fixed algebra would satisfy 2φ(p) ≤ φ(q) ≤ φ(p), hence φ(p)=φ(q)=0, contradicting φ(p+q)=1.

statement: [arXiv:2107.02917 Lemma 3.6] Let Γ be a finite simplicial graph, let {G_v : v ∈ V(Γ)} be a family of countable groups, and let G = Γ(G_v) be the graph product. For a vertex v, write S_v = {w ∈ V(Γ) : (v,w) ∈ E(Γ)} for its link, and write H_v = Γ_{S_v}(G_v) for the full graph-product subgroup generated by the vertex groups in the link of v, with the convention that the graph product over the empty set is the trivial group. For any subgroup H ≤ G, let X_H be the closed left-and-right G-invariant part of the Stone-Cech boundary βG \ G corresponding to the quotient ℓ∞(G)/c_0(G,H). Then the link boundary pieces cover the whole Stone-Cech boundary: ⋃_{v ∈ V(Γ)} X_{H_v} = βG \ G. Equivalently, ⋂_{v ∈ V(Γ)} c_0(G,H_v) = c_0(G).
proof_sketch: Enumerate V(Γ)={v1,...,vn} and put Γ_i=Γ_{S_{v_i}}, H_i=Γ_i(G). It is enough to prove that for arbitrary s_i,t_i ∈ G the intersection ⋂_{i=1}^n s_i H_i t_i is finite. Indeed, if f lies in every c_0(G,H_i) but not in c_0(G), then for some ε>0 the set A={g: |f(g)|≥ε} is infinite. Since f ∈ c_0(G,H_1), A cannot escape H_1, so an infinite subset of A lies in some double coset s_1H_1t_1; iterating over i gives an infinite subset of A inside ⋂ s_iH_it_i, contradicting finiteness. The finiteness of such intersections is obtained from Lemma 3.5 of the paper: for full subgroups Γ_A(G), Γ_B(G) and fixed g,h ∈ G, the intersection Γ_A(G) ∩ g Γ_B(G) h is contained in a finite union of double translates c_j Γ_{A∩B}(G) d_j. The proof of Lemma 3.5 is a normal-form induction. Write g and h as fixed reduced graph-product words and write an element of Γ_A(G) ∩ gΓ_B(G)h as g t h with t ∈ Γ_B(G). Any letter of t whose vertex is not in A∩B must disappear in the reduction of gth because the final element lies in Γ_A(G). Such disappearance can occur only by commuting that letter past adjacent letters until it cancels with one of finitely many letters coming from the fixed words g,h; hence the possible outside letters are accounted for by finitely many subwords of g,h. Removing one outside vertex type lowers the number of letters of t outside A∩B, giving the induction and the finite union. Applying this containment repeatedly to ⋂ s_iH_it_i gives a finite union of double translates of Γ_{⋂_i S_{v_i}}(G). But ⋂_{v∈V(Γ)} S_v is empty, since each vertex v is not in its own link S_v. Hence Γ_{⋂_i S_{v_i}}(G) is the trivial group, so every such intersection of link-subgroup double cosets is finite. This proves ⋂ c_0(G,H_v)=c_0(G), equivalently the boundary cover.

statement: [arXiv:2107.02917 Proposition 3.7] Let Γ be a finite simplicial graph. For vertices u,w, let d(u,w) be the length of the shortest path in Γ, with d(u,w)=∞ if no path exists, and let the radius be r(Γ)=inf_{v∈V(Γ)} sup_{w∈V(Γ)} d(v,w). The following are equivalent. (1) r(Γ) ≥ 2, and there do not exist two distinct vertices v1,v2 ∈ V(Γ) such that, for each i=1,2 and every u ∈ V(Γ) \ {v1,v2}, the edge (u,v_i) belongs to E(Γ). (2) For every choice of countable nontrivial groups {G_v : v ∈ V(Γ)}, the graph product Γ(G_v) is properly proximal.
proof_sketch: For (1)⇒(2), fix nontrivial vertex groups and put G=Γ(G_v). For each vertex v, let H_v=Γ_{S_v}(G), A_v=Γ_{S_v∪{v}}(G), and B_v=Γ_{V\{v}}(G). Lemma 2.4 gives the canonical amalgamated free product decomposition G = A_v *_ {H_v} B_v. Since v commutes with every vertex in S_v, A_v=H_v×G_v, so [A_v:H_v]=|G_v|≥2. The radius condition r(Γ)≥2 says v is never adjacent to all other vertices, hence B_v is strictly larger than H_v and [B_v:H_v]≥2. The additional no-pair condition rules out the only case in which both indices could be exactly 2: this would require |G_v|=2 and B_v=H_v×G_w for a single order-two vertex w outside S_v that is adjacent to every vertex of S_v, making v and w adjacent to all vertices outside {v,w}. Thus for every v, after possibly swapping A_v and B_v, the AFP indices satisfy the hypotheses of Corollary 3.2, and G is properly proximal relative to H_v. Lemma 3.6 says the closed boundary pieces X_{H_v} cover βG\G. Relative proper proximality for each H_v plus this finite cover implies proper proximality of G: if a left G-invariant state existed on the full proximal algebra over βG\G, then some closed invariant piece X_{H_v} would have positive mass; normalizing the restriction would give a left G-invariant state on the relative proximal algebra for H_v, contradicting relative proper proximality. For (2)⇒(1), the paper exhibits choices of vertex groups that obstruct proper proximality whenever (1) fails. If Γ has a universal vertex v, choose G_v to be an infinite amenable group; then the graph product splits as G_v × Γ_{V\{v}}(G), giving an infinite amenable direct factor, which is incompatible with proper proximality. If r(Γ)=2 but there are vertices v1,v2 adjacent to every vertex outside {v1,v2}, then v1 and v2 are not adjacent to each other; choosing G_{v1}=G_{v2}=Z/2Z gives a direct product (Z/2Z * Z/2Z) × Γ_{V\{v1,v2}}(G). The first factor is the infinite dihedral group, hence amenable and inner amenable; the resulting graph product is not properly proximal. The observation following the proposition notes that irreducible finite graphs, equivalently graphs with connected complement, automatically satisfy condition (1) when they have at least three vertices: a universal vertex or such a pair would leave isolated vertices in the complement.

statement: For a fixed vertex v, with B=M_Link(v), A=M_Star(v), C=M_{V\setminus{v}}, the graph product M=A *_B C is properly proximal relative to the DKEP boundary piece X_B generated by e_B.
proof_sketch: Sector projections for the AFP first-letter decomposition lie in S_{X_B}(M); ping-pong unitaries force incompatible inequalities for any M-central state.

statement: The projections R_A, R_C, P_A=R_A-e_B, and P_C=R_C-e_B belong to S_{X_B}(M).
proof_sketch: Right multiplication changes the first-letter sector only through terms e_B rho(x) and rho(x)e_B, which factor through L^2(B) and hence lie in K^{∞,1}_{X_B}.

statement: There exist unitaries a in A and c_1,c_2 in C with E_B(a)=0, E_B(c_i)=0, and E_B(c_i^*c_j)=delta_ij 1.
proof_sketch: Take a from M_v; for c_i use either two non-neighbours of v, or in the unique non-neighbour case use irreducibility to find a nonadjacent z in Link(v) and set c_1=u_w, c_2=u_z u_w.

statement: The exact containments c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^* <= P_C and aR_Ca^* <= P_A hold, with no e_B-error.
proof_sketch: Left multiplication by a C⊖B unitary sends H_A^+ into the C-sector, and left multiplication by an A⊖B unitary sends H_C^+ into the A-sector; B-valued orthogonality gives orthogonal ranges.

statement: If M is not properly proximal and has no amenable summand, then there is an M-central normal-bidual state Omega on the absolute compactification with Omega restricted to M equal to the trace and Omega(q_K)=0.
proof_sketch: This is DKEP Lemma 8.6 for the trivial action, with the weakly compact alternative excluded by nonamenability.

statement: If M is properly proximal relative to a boundary piece X, then any absolute obstruction state Omega as above satisfies Omega(q_X)=1.
proof_sketch: If Omega(1-q_X)>0, compressing by 1-q_X sends the relative compactification into the absolute one and gives an M-central state normal on M, contradicting relative proper proximality.

statement: For graph-product subalgebras M_U, the DKEP support projections satisfy q_U1 meet ... meet q_Un = q_{U1 cap ... cap Un}.
proof_sketch: The reduced-word conditional expectation formula shows that products of the hereditary cores generated by e_{M_U} and e_{M_W} factor through e_{M_{U cap W}} modulo the DKEP strong compact closure; approximate-unit and state-separation then give the support meet formula.

statement: For U empty, q_U is the compact support q_K.
proof_sketch: The projection e_{M_empty} is the rank-one projection onto C1, and its M-JMJ hereditary hull is K(L2M).

statement: If M is properly proximal relative to X_{M_Link(v)} for every vertex v of a finite graph product, then M is properly proximal.
proof_sketch: An absolute obstruction state would have mass one on every link support, hence by the intersection formula mass one on q_empty=q_K, contradicting the construction of the obstruction state with Omega(q_K)=0.

approach: Naive norm product X_U X_W subset X_{U cap W}
reason: The DKEP boundary piece X_U contains the large corner e_U B(L2M) e_U, so a direct norm-product inclusion is too strong; the correct argument uses the hereditary core, reduced-word finite propagation, and DKEP strong-bimodule closure.

statement: DKEP's boundary piece associated to B subset M is the hereditary C*-subalgebra X_B generated by x J y J e_B, and DKEP Lemmas 8.5 and 8.6 provide a normal-bidual criterion for relative proper proximality using the support of K_X.

statement: For a fixed vertex v in an irreducible finite graph product satisfying the trace-zero-unitary hypothesis, the graph product von Neumann algebra is properly proximal relative to the DKEP boundary piece associated with M_Link(v).
The fixed-vertex AFP ping-pong lemma from t1 is valid under the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility.
proof_sketch: The graph product splits as M_Star(v) amalgamated over M_Link(v) with M_{V minus {v}}; the first-letter sector projections have only link-relative compact commutators; graph irreducibility supplies the required B-orthogonal unitaries; the resulting projection inequalities contradict any M-central state.

statement: The unique non-neighbor and finite-dimensional vertex cases in t1 are handled correctly.
proof_sketch: If w is the unique non-neighbor of v, irreducibility gives z in Link(v) not adjacent to w; then u_w, u_z u_w are B-orthogonal, and the word u_w^* u_z u_w cannot reduce even when u_w has order two.

statement: The nonamenability/no amenable summand step in t2 can be repaired using t1.
proof_sketch: An amenable direct summand would provide a hypertrace, which by central compression gives an M-central state on the relative system S_X for any link, contradicting the fixed-vertex ping-pong lemma.

approach: Nonamenability via free Haar unitaries in two nonadjacent vertex algebras
reason: This fails for finite-dimensional vertices such as C^2, where two trace-zero order-two unitaries generate the amenable algebra L(Z2 * Z2).

approach: Support-intersection proof using arbitrary states and approximate units
reason: Nonnormal states need not see approximate units of a hereditary subalgebra even when they take value one on its support projection.

reason: The sector projections are generally only link-relative, not absolute compact, unless the link algebra is finite-dimensional or trivial.

statement: For all induced vertex sets U,W in a finite tracial graph product, the DKEP support projections satisfy q_U q_W = q_W q_U = q_{U cap W}; hence q_U meet q_W = q_{U cap W}.
proof_sketch: Use DKEP approximate units built from algebraic conjugates of e_U. A reduced-word finite-propagation lemma shows h_U h_W h_U is in K_{U cap W}^{infty,1}; passing to the normal bidual gives q_U q_W q_U <= q_{U cap W}, and the reverse support inclusion forces equality and commutation.

statement: The finite meet formula q_{U_1} meet ... meet q_{U_n} = q_{U_1 cap ... cap U_n} holds.

statement: If M has no amenable summand and is not properly proximal, there is an absolute M-central obstruction state Omega with Omega(q_K)=0; if M is properly proximal relative to X_U, then Omega(q_U)=1.

M = A *_{B} C along B = M_{Link(v)}, one uses the DKEP generator criterion to show that the canonical Jones projection e_B and the star–link projections R_A,R_C (hence P_A=P_A−e_B, P_C=R_C−e_B) all lie in the boundary operator system S_{X_B}(M). proof_excerpt: They exploit the Caspers–Fima “star–link’’ amalgamated free‐product decomposition M=A*_B C over B=M_{Link(v)} to show that the Jones projections and the reduced‐sector projections F_A,F_C,P_A,P_C all lie in the DKEP operator system S_{X_Link(v)}, since their commutators with right M‐multipliers fall into the DKEP ideal.  One then picks a centered unitary a∈A° and two orthogonal‐in‐B unitaries c₁,c₂∈C° and uses their left‐multiplication action on these projections to run a ping-pong argument that forces any putative M-central state on S_{X_Link(v)} to satisfy impossible linear inequalities, ruling out its existence and hence giving proper proximality.  Finally, a standard central‐summand/hypertrace construction shows that M cannot admit a nonzero amenable direct summand.

\[

by a finite‐propagation argument for Jones projections in the graph product.  Iterating this across all vertex‐links forces \(\Omega\) to put full mass on the empty support (hence on the compact ideal), contradicting its vanishing there and concluding proper proximality.

statement: Let Γ be a finite simplicial graph with at least three vertices, irreducible in the standard nontrivial join sense, and let (M_v, τ_v) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras such that each M_v contains a unitary u_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra M_Γ is properly proximal.
problem_solved: After inserting the listed routine patches, the proof establishes the claimed theorem under the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility: for a finite simple loopless irreducible graph \(\Gamma\) with \(|V|\ge3\), if each separable tracial vertex algebra \(M_v\) contains a trace-zero unitary, then the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra \(\star_\Gamma(M_v,\tau_v)\) is properly proximal.

statement:
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.
